sutta: Thus have I heard: On a certain occasion, the Exalted One was staying near Savatthi at Jeta Grove in Anathapindika's Park.
commentary: All the suttas start with these words, 'Thus have I heard.' This is the Buddhist tradition decided at the First Sangha Council. 'The Exalted One' is a translation of Bhagavā. Bhagavā is one of the ten names of the Buddha. This monastery was donated by Anathapindika.

sutta: Thus have I heard: On a certain occasion, the Exalted One was staying near Savatthi at Jeta Grove in Anathapindika's Park. The Exalted One addressed the monks: 'Monks, I know of no other single form by which a man's heart is so enslaved as it is by that of a woman; a woman's form obsesses a man's heart. Monks, I know of no other single sound... scent... taste... touch by which a man's heart is so enslaved as it is by that of a woman. Monks, I know of no other single form by which a woman's heart is so enslaved as it is by that of a man; a man's form obsesses a woman's heart.'
commentary: Form (Rupa) is one of the five sense objects. The opposite sex is the strongest object of sensual attraction. These five are the objects of the senses; they are the objects of sensual desire. This is why chastity is required in spiritual paths. Many monks disrobe due to sensual desire. The Buddha warned monks about proximity to nuns; when Ananda asked how to deal with women, the Buddha said: 'Do not look. If you must look, do not speak. If you must speak, do so with mindfulness.' Monks depend on laypeople and cannot isolate completely, which creates danger like a deer near a farmer (a simile for Māra). Only strong Samādhi protects the monk; without the higher pleasure of Pīti and Sukha, one remains attached to sensual pleasure.

sutta: Monks, I know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of sensual desire... as a feature of beauty (Subha-nimitta) in him who pays not thorough attention. ...I know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of ill will... as a repulsive feature in him who pays not thorough attention. ...I know not of any other single thing of such power to cause the arising of sloth and torpor... as regret, drowsiness, languor, fatigue after meals, and stupidity of mind.
commentary: Sensual desire arises due to the perception of attractiveness. This is an arbitrary perception formed in the mind. The Buddha calls the body, feeling, perception, volition, and consciousness the five aggregates (Panchakhanda). Perceptions can change. By cultivating the 32 parts of the body, one sees the body as it really is (blood, bones, flesh), and the attraction diminishes. Yoniso Manasikara means 'thorough attention'—Yoni (source) and Manasikara (work of the mind). It is attention that brings you back to the source of the problem. Mettā is the antidote to anger; it requires strong concentration. To fight sloth, one needs effort; too much sleep increases sloth. The antidote to restlessness is Samatha (tranquility). Doubt is resolved through reflection and testing the teachings.

sutta: Monks, I know not of any other single thing so uncontrollable as the uncultivated mind... so controllable as the cultivated mind... so conducive to great loss as the uncultivated mind... so conducive to great profit as the cultivated mind.
commentary: In Samatha meditation, we exercise control. Every time the mind runs, we bring it back. Samādhi is the result of this control. There are two aspects of cultivation (Bhavana): Samatha (to train/control) and Vipassana (to use that controlled mind to contemplate wisdom). An uncultivated mind leads to loss because hindrances are strong, leading to wrong decisions. A cultivated mind leads to profit through wisdom. Jhāna literally means brightness or incandescence; when the mind is bright, it is the best condition for insight.

sutta: Suppose, monks, a pool of water—lucid, tranquil, and unstirred. A man standing on the bank could see the oysters, shells, pebbles, and shoals of fish. Why? Because of the untroubled nature of the water. Just so, it is possible for a monk with an untroubled mind to understand his own profit and that of others.
commentary: The troubled/untroubled state refers to the five hindrances. Similes: Sensual desire is water with dye; Ill will is boiling water; Sloth is water covered with weeds; Restlessness is water stirred by wind; Doubt is muddy water in a dark place. Clear, still water is like a mind with strong Samādhi.

sutta: Monks, I know not of any other single thing so quick to change as the mind.
commentary: Because the mind changes extremely fast, we must use it to our advantage. If a good intention arises, act quickly before the mind changes. If a bad intention (like anger) arises, do nothing. Wait for it to subside. Using the changing nature of the mind is wisdom.

sutta: This mind, monks, is luminous, but it is defiled by things that come from without. That mind, monks, is luminous, but it is cleansed of things that come from without.
commentary: Citta (mind) is distinct from Vinyana (consciousness) and Mano (thinking faculty). The mind is originally bright, but defilements are superimposed. Some interpret this as an 'unsupported consciousness' that does not depend on objects. Thai Forest teachers speak of the original mind as bright; energy is usually scattered through the six senses, causing darkness. Through Samādhi/Jhāna, the mind becomes one-pointed and regains luminosity. Nibbana is beyond existence or non-existence; it is experienced, not known by the ordinary mind.
